concepts.title: Why the distance defines the model

beats.text: k-NN, k-means, DBSCAN, and every vector index are not really algorithms over data; each is an algorithm over a distance.
A function counts as a true metric only if it satisfies four properties, and breaking any one makes geometric intuition untrustworthy.
Euclidean, Manhattan, and Mahalanobis satisfy all four axioms, while cosine and squared-Euclidean distance break the triangle inequality.
A distance combines coordinates, so it implicitly weights them; a feature in kilometres silently dominates one in millimetres.
Before any distance is computed you z-score each column to mean zero and variance one, or min-max scale to the unit interval.

visual.label: target mean per feature after z-scoring

beats.text: Trees are the exception that proves the rule, caring only about the order of values within a column, so they never need rescaling.

visual.text: The distance is the inductive bias

beats.text: The workhorse distances are a single formula with one dial, the order p, that reshapes what counts as near.
Minkowski distance raises each coordinate gap to the power p, sums them, and takes the p-th root.

visual.note: p=1 Manhattan, p=2 Euclidean, p to infinity Chebyshev

beats.text: For points (1,1) and (4,5) the gaps are 3 and 4, giving Manhattan 7, Euclidean 5, and Chebyshev 4.
Higher p always gives a smaller or equal distance, so the three readings fall in a fixed order.
The unit ball, the set of points at distance one, becomes a diamond for Manhattan, a circle for Euclidean, and a square for Chebyshev.
Because two points equidistant under one p need not be under another, the nearest neighbour itself can change with p.
Euclidean distance treats every direction as equal and every feature as independent, but real data rarely obliges.

visual.sub: Measuring in units of the data's own spread

beats.text: Mahalanobis distance uses the inverse covariance to deflate high-variance directions and inflate low-variance ones.

visual.note: Sigma is the covariance; Sigma inverse is the precision matrix

beats.text: When the covariance is the identity, with uncorrelated unit-variance features, Mahalanobis collapses back to plain Euclidean distance.
It is just Euclidean distance computed after whitening, applying the transform that decorrelates features and rescales each to unit variance.
With variances 9 and 16 and a gap of (9,16), the squared distance is 9 plus 16, so d-M equals the square root of 25.
For Gaussian data the squared distance follows a chi-square law, giving a principled threshold for outlier and anomaly detection.
Sometimes magnitude is noise and only direction carries meaning, so two documents in the same proportions are about the same thing.
Cosine similarity normalizes both vectors to unit length and takes their dot product, the cosine of the angle between them.
On L2-normalized vectors squared Euclidean distance equals two times one minus cosine, so cosine ranking equals Euclidean ranking.

visual.note: Why embedding pipelines normalize before storing

beats.text: Jaccard similarity measures set overlap, the size of the intersection over the size of the union of two sets.

visual.tex: J(A,B) = \frac{\lvert A \cap B \rvert}{\lvert A \cup B \rvert}, \qquad d_J(A,B) = 1 - J(A,B)
visual.note: 1 identical, 0 disjoint; the 1 minus J distance is a true metric

beats.text: For A equals 1,2,3 and B equals 2,3,4 the intersection has size 2 and the union has size 4, so Jaccard is one half.
Of these five measures only cosine fails the metric test, while Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis, and one minus Jaccard are true metrics.
Everything rests on a quiet assumption, that nearest is meaningfully different from farthest, which quietly fails as dimensions grow.

visual.sub: When near and far become the same

beats.text: For independent coordinates the relative contrast between the farthest and nearest points of a query vanishes as dimension grows.
Each new coordinate adds roughly equal independent noise, so all distances pile up around the same mean and their relative spread collapses.
In a 100-dimensional unit cube a sub-cube holding just one percent of the volume needs edge length about 0.955.

visual.label: edge length to capture 1% of points at d=100

beats.text: Lower p concentrates more slowly, so Manhattan is often the better choice than Euclidean in high dimensions.
Real data escapes the curse by living near a low-dimensional manifold, which is why k-NN on a learned embedding still works.
